verses.commentary: I commend to you our sister Phoebe, a deacon of the church in Cenchreae. Commend (synistēmi, συνίστημι, introduce, recommend) Phoebe. Sister affirms her faith. Deacon (diakonos, διάκονος) may mean servant or formal office. Church in Cenchreae (Corinth's eastern port). Phoebe likely carried this letter to Rome. Paul honors her service.

I ask you to receive her in the Lord in a way worthy of his people and to give her any help she may need from you, for she has been the benefactor of many people, including me. Receive (prosdechomai, προσδέχομαι, welcome) her in the Lord (as a believer). Worthy of the saints means with honor and support. Give help (paristēmi, παρίστημι, stand by, assist) as needed. Benefactor (prostatis, προστάτις, patron, helper) describes Phoebe's role: she supported many, including Paul. She deserves reciprocal care.

Greet Priscilla and Aquila, my co-workers in Christ Jesus. Priscilla (Prisca) and Aquila: husband and wife, tentmakers, Paul's ministry partners (Acts 18). Co-workers (synergos, συνεργός) emphasizes shared labor. In Christ Jesus marks spiritual partnership.

They risked their lives for me. Not only I but all the churches of the Gentiles are grateful to them. Risked their lives (hypetithēmi ton trachēlon, ὑπέθηκαν τὸν τράχηλον, laid down their necks) is sacrificial service. When or how is unknown. All Gentile churches are grateful. Their influence was widespread. Faithful service benefits the whole body.

Greet also the church that meets at their house. Greet my dear friend Epenetus, who was the first convert to Christ in the province of Asia. House church: believers met in homes. Epenetus: first convert (aparchē, ἀπαρχή, firstfruits) in Asia. Firsts are significant; pioneers hold special honor.

Greet Mary, who worked very hard for you. Mary: otherwise unknown. Worked hard (kopiaō, κοπιάω, toil, labor to exhaustion) describes sacrificial service. Many women served prominently in the early church.

Greet Andronicus and Junia, my fellow Jews who have been in prison with me. They are outstanding among the apostles, and they were in Christ before I was. Andronicus and Junia (likely wife): Jewish believers, imprisoned with Paul. Outstanding among the apostles could mean esteemed by apostles or part of the apostolic circle. In Christ before Paul suggests early converts. Length of faith is not superiority, but it is noteworthy.
Greet Ampliatus, my dear friend in the Lord. Ampliatus: loved (agapētos, ἀγαπητός) by Paul. In the Lord qualifies all Christian relationships. Friendship in Christ is eternal.
Greet Urbanus, our co-worker in Christ, and my dear friend Stachys. Urbanus: co-worker. Stachys: beloved. These are personal, affectionate greetings. Paul values relationships deeply.

Greet Apelles, whose fidelity to Christ has stood the test. Greet those who belong to the household of Aristobulus. Apelles: tested (dokimos, δόκιμος, approved, proven). His fidelity (faith) has been tested and found genuine. Household of Aristobulus: possibly slaves or family members. Aristocratic households had many members.

Greet Herodion, my fellow Jew. Greet those in the household of Narcissus who are in the Lord. Herodion: fellow Jew (syngenes, συγγενής, kinsman). Household of Narcissus: likely a prominent Roman household. In the Lord distinguishes believers in the household; not all were Christians.

Greet Tryphena and Tryphosa, those women who work hard in the Lord. Greet my dear friend Persis, another woman who has worked very hard in the Lord. Tryphena and Tryphosa: likely sisters, both laborers in the Lord. Persis: another hard worker (kopiaō, κοπιάω). Beloved (agapētos, ἀγαπητός). Paul honors women's ministry extensively. The early church valued women's contributions.

Greet Rufus, chosen in the Lord, and his mother, who has been a mother to me, too. Rufus: chosen (eklektos, ἐκλεκτός, elect) in the Lord (possibly Mark 15:21's Rufus). His mother cared for Paul maternally. Christian community creates family bonds beyond biology.

Greet Asyncritus, Phlegon, Hermes, Patrobas, Hermas and the other brothers and sisters with them. Five men named, plus others. Likely a house church. Brothers and sisters (adelphoi, ἀδελφοί) emphasizes family.

Greet Philologus, Julia, Nereus and his sister, and Olympas and all the Lord's people who are with them. Another group, likely another house church. Julia may be Philologus's wife. Nereus and his sister (unnamed). Olympas. All the Lord's people (hagioi, ἅγιοι, saints) encompasses the whole group.

Greet one another with a holy kiss. All the churches of Christ send greetings. Holy kiss: a cultural greeting, sanctified by Christian use. It expressed love and unity. All the churches send greetings through Paul. This connects Roman believers to the universal church.

I urge you, brothers and sisters, to watch out for those who cause divisions and put obstacles in your way that are contrary to the teaching you have learned. Keep away from them. A warning. Watch out (skopeō, σκοπέω, observe, mark) for divisive people. Divisions (dichostasia, διχοστασία, dissensions) and obstacles (skandalon, σκάνδαλον, stumbling blocks) are contrary to apostolic teaching. Keep away (ekklinō, ἐκκλίνω, turn away from) them. False teachers must be avoided.

For such people are not serving our Lord Christ, but their own appetites. By smooth talk and flattery they deceive the minds of naive people. Their master: not Christ but their own belly (koilia, κοιλία, appetites). Smooth talk (chrēstologia, χρηστολογία, fine speech) and flattery (eulogia, εὐλογία, good words) deceive the naive (akakos, ἄκακος, innocent, unsuspecting). False teachers use rhetoric to mislead. Discernment is critical.

Everyone has heard about your obedience, so I rejoice because of you; but I want you to be wise about what is good, and innocent about what is evil. The Romans' obedience is famous. Rejoice (chairō, χαίρω). But: be wise (sophos, σοφός) about good and innocent (akeraios, ἀκέραιος, unmixed, pure) about evil. Know good thoroughly; avoid evil completely. Wisdom discerns; innocence protects.

The God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet. The grace of our Lord Jesus be with you. God of peace (ironic: peace comes through crushing Satan). Crush (syntribō, συντρίβω, shatter) echoes Genesis 3:15. Soon (en tachei, ἐν τάχει, speedily) promises imminent victory. Under your feet: believers share in Satan's defeat. Grace (charis, χάρις) benediction. Victory over evil is certain.

Timothy, my co-worker, sends his greetings to you, as do Lucius, Jason and Sosipater, my fellow Jews. Timothy: Paul's protege, co-worker (synergos, συνεργός). Lucius, Jason, Sosipater: fellow Jews (syngenes, συγγενής). These send greetings from Paul's location (likely Corinth). Ministry is collaborative.

I, Tertius, who wrote down this letter, greet you in the Lord. Tertius: Paul's scribe (amanuensis). He penned Romans as Paul dictated. In the Lord marks him as a believer. This personal touch humanizes the letter. Paul often used secretaries (Romans, 1 Corinthians, Galatians).

Gaius, whose hospitality I and the whole church here enjoy, sends you his greetings. Erastus, who is the city's director of public works, and our brother Quartus send you their greetings. Gaius: host to Paul and the church (likely 1 Corinthians 1:14's Gaius). Hospitality (xenia, ξενία) is valued. Erastus: city official (oikonomos, οἰκονόμος, treasurer or manager). Christians in government. Quartus: otherwise unknown. The church includes people from all social levels.

Some manuscripts include: The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all. Amen. This verse is textually disputed (many manuscripts omit it, others include it here or after v. 27). If original, it is a repeated grace benediction. Grace bookends the letter (1:7, 16:24).

Now to Him who is able to establish you in accordance with my gospel, the message I proclaim about Jesus Christ, in keeping with the revelation of the mystery hidden for long ages past. Doxology begins. To Him shifts focus to God. Able to establish (stērizō, στηρίζω, strengthen, confirm). According to Paul's gospel: the message about Jesus Christ. Revelation of the mystery (mystērion, μυστήριον): once hidden, now revealed. Mystery: Gentile inclusion in God's people, salvation by faith. Hidden for ages (chronois aiōniois, χρόνοις αἰωνίοις, eternal times) but now disclosed.

But now revealed and made known through the prophetic writings by the command of the eternal God, so that all the Gentiles might come to the obedience that comes from faith. Now revealed (phaneroō, φανερόω, made manifest). Through prophetic writings (Old Testament pointed forward). By command of eternal God: divine authority. Purpose: all Gentiles come to obedience of faith (hypakoē pisteōs, ὑπακοὴ πίστεως). Faith produces obedience; the gospel is for all nations. The mystery is now universal.

To the only wise God be glory forever through Jesus Christ! Amen. The climax: to the only wise God. Glory (doxa, δόξα) belongs to Him alone. Forever (eis tous aiōnas, εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας, unto the ages). Through Jesus Christ: all glory comes through the Son. Amen (so be it) seals the doxology and the letter. Romans ends as it began: with the glory of God in the gospel of Jesus Christ.
